findings.description: _is_expired is inverted: `return age < self.ttl` reports True (expired) for FRESH entries and False for OLD ones, so in get() fresh L1 entries are deleted (lines 53-54) while genuinely stale entries are served as valid hits (lines 55-57) — the cache serves stale data and L1 provides zero benefit; correct check is `age >= self.ttl`.

The L2/Redis read path (lines 58-63) performs no TTL/expiry validation — it returns whatever Redis holds unconditionally; combined with the in-scope RedisClient.setex (line 29) ignoring ttl_seconds and never expiring keys, the module's documented 'TTL-based expiry' contract is fully unmet for any value that resides in L2, so stale data can be returned indefinitely.

Hit/miss accounting is corrupted by the inverted expiry check: an entry that has actually expired takes the else-branch and is counted as a hit (line 56) instead of leading to a miss, so CacheStats.hits/misses (and thus export_stats output) are systematically wrong whenever entries age past the TTL.

For a cache layer in a web-service codebase (concurrency strongly implied), self._local and self.stats are mutated without any lock — concurrent get() calls can interleave the `del self._local[key]` (line 54) with reads/writes and produce KeyError or lost-update races on the stats counters; no synchronization primitive is present.

warm() returns len(entries) (the input size) but its docstring promises 'the number of entries written'; these coincide only because set() is currently infallible — if set() ever gains failure modes or skips duplicates the returned count becomes a silent lie; consider counting successful writes.

pickle.loads on bytes fetched from Redis (line 60) deserializes untrusted/poisonable data and enables arbitrary code execution if the Redis store is compromised or shared — flagged in passing per logic-only scope; defer full analysis to critic-security.
